result.chapter_summary: The decline in American verbal SAT scores since the 1940s is the result of a fundamental shift in educational philosophy from Enlightenment-based 'teacher-centered' models to Romantic-based 'child-centered' ones. This shift is characterized by the change in dominant metaphors from the 'Mirror' (induction into a shared national culture) to the 'Lamp' (the expression of innate individual genius).

claims.text: The decline in verbal SAT scores for college entrance is a consequence of the elementary school revolution that began in the 1940s.
claims.source_passage: The chart of our decline in verbal SAT scores for college entrance traces the consequence of the elementary school revolution that started in the 1940s.

claims.text: American education moved from a teacher-centered model to a child-centered model starting in the 1940s.
claims.source_passage: In that decade our move from teacher-centered to child-centered education started off in a big way.

claims.text: The 1940s sentiment of national unity and confidence encouraged the adoption of naturalistic, child-centered education.
claims.source_passage: In that more confident mood, we started instituting naturalistic, child-centered education on a big scale.

claims.text: American schooling departed from its Enlightenment roots to adopt a Romantic approach to education in the 1940s.
claims.source_passage: It was then that American schooling departed from its intellectual roots in John Locke and the Enlightenment to adopt the Romantic approach to education.

claims.text: The shift from the Enlightenment to the Romantic tradition is characterized by a move from accurately mirroring the world to illuminating it with individual genius.
claims.source_passage: It described the literary shift from accurately mirroring the world as it is to illuminating the world with the lamp of one’s God-given individual genius.

claims.text: The Enlightenment tradition offered children an explicit induction into national citizenship, functioning as a 'mirror.'
claims.source_passage: Our Enlightenment educational tradition had offered the child a direct, explicit induction into the language and the mores of national citizenship—a mirror.

claims.text: A 'mirror' approach to education is necessary to master a national print language and achieve high verbal SAT scores.
claims.source_passage: And a mirror is what is needed to master a national print language and earn high grades on the Verbal SAT.

claims.text: Shared knowledge is imparted most effectively through whole-class instruction using commonly shared materials.
claims.source_passage: Such shared knowledge is imparted most effectively through whole-class instruction in a sequence of commonly shared materials, not from helter-skelter child-chosen booklets from the classroom library.

claims.text: Whole-class instruction is the fairest and most effective way to teach a shared national language and background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: whole-class instruction is by far the fairest and most effective way to teach children the shared national language with its shared background knowledge.

claims.text: Teaching shared national knowledge instills shared loyalty to American traditions and fellow citizens.
claims.source_passage: To do so instills shared loyalty to American traditions and to fellow citizens.

claims.text: The mastery of shared language in early grades enables effective individuality in later years.
claims.source_passage: Paradoxically, the mastery of shared language in early grades enables an effective individuality later on, not to mention desirably high SAT scores.

claims.text: The progressive-education idea is based on 19th-century Romanticism and its metaphor of the seedling.
claims.source_passage: The later “progressive-education” idea was based on 19th-century romanticism. Its metaphor was that of a seedling ready to unfold.

claims.text: Romanticism resuscitated the idea of innate ideas which had been rejected by Enlightenment thinkers like Locke and Hume.
claims.source_passage: But innate ideas were resuscitated by the romantics—including by John Dewey... That seedling metaphor for the human mind had been rejected by Locke and Hume who discarded the old notion of innate ideas.

claims.text: Romantic education theory posits that learning is chiefly a natural growth from within fueled by innate impulses rather than instruction from the outside.
claims.source_passage: Education was not to be just receptive instruction from the outside—a mirror. It was chiefly to be a natural growth from within. It was a God-given lamp that is fueled by the natural, innate impulses of the individual child.

claims.text: Child-centered education is underpinned by an implicit religious premise, accounting for the fervor of its advocates.
claims.source_passage: There still persists an implicit religious premise in child-centered education-which helps account for the fervor of its advocates.

claims.text: Post-1940s romantic educators characterized education using botanical images and metaphors.
claims.source_passage: post-1940s romantic educators generously applied botanical images and metaphors.

claims.text: The romantic educational view regards external interference with a child's natural growth as artificial and destructive.
claims.source_passage: The romantics said that anything which interfered with the child’s authentic, natural growth was “artificial” and destructive of God’s implicit (seeded) plan for each individual child.

claims.text: Romanticism replaced Enlightenment ideas as the dominant framework for American schooling after the 1940s.
claims.source_passage: After the 1940s in the United States, Romanticism increasingly replaced our earlier Enlightenment ideas about schooling.

claims.text: The Romantic premise of education favors nature and instinct over the 'meddling intellect.'
claims.source_passage: Romanticism’s counter premise was that Nature and Instinct know better than Locke’s ”meddling intellect” about how to school a child.

claims.text: There is no natural or biological way for a child to acquire literacy or cultural knowledge without instruction.
claims.source_passage: But despite its claim that “we’ve gone from A to Z doing what comes naturally,” there’s no natural-growth way to get from A to Z-either literally or metaphorically.

claims.text: Written alphabets and systems of writing are cultural artifacts rather than natural developments.
claims.source_passage: Our Roman alphabet is no more natural than the Greek alphabet, or the Hebrew, Persian, or Chinese systems of writing. To go “from A to Z” you must emphatically do what comes culturally.

claims.text: The particulars of human culture are not inborn or innate.
claims.source_passage: The word “nature” means inborn, innate, and that’s simply false for the particularities of any particular human culture.

claims.text: John Locke's blank slate metaphor is scientifically accurate regarding the acquisition of literacy and cultural lore.
claims.source_passage: Locke was right about the blank slate, at least with regard to literacy and the learning of tribal lore. Once we acknowledge that scientific truth, perhaps we will be sensible enough to go back to his metaphor of the blank slate for most of our schooling.

claims.text: Re-adopting the blank slate metaphor for schooling will increase national unity and patriotism.
claims.source_passage: And so will our unity and patriotism, and the level of our politics.

claims.text: The transition from teacher-centered to child-centered education involved a physical shift in classroom furniture from desks in rows facing the teacher to scattered tables where students face each other.
claims.source_passage: From the 1940s to the 1960s there was a gradual movement of the classroom furniture from student desks facing the teacher, to scattered tables and moveable chairs, with the children facing each other.

claims.text: The contemporary United States is characterized by national disunity and debased politics, which contrast with the unity and confidence of the 1940s.
claims.source_passage: Our current feelings of national disunity and our debased politics contrast sharply with our earlier 1940s sense of unity and confidence.

claims.text: The United States' role as the 'arsenal of democracy' and its development of the atomic bomb in the 1940s contributed to a national mood where History and Nature seemed to be on the nation's side.
claims.source_passage: During the wartime 1940s, the United States had developed the atom bomb and had become the arsenal of democracy. Both History and Nature seemed on our side.

claims.text: John Dewey, a primary influence on modern schooling, is specifically categorized as a Hegel-Darwin Romantic.
claims.source_passage: But innate ideas were resuscitated by the romantics—including by John Dewey, our most influential modern school theorist, who was a Hegel-Darwin Romantic

claims.text: The words 'innate' and 'nature' are etymologically connected, which reinforces the Romantic metaphor of the mind as a seedling growing according to its internal nature.
claims.source_passage: A plant grows according to its innate nature. (Note the etymological connection of the two words innate and nature.)

claims.text: To achieve literacy (moving from 'A to Z'), one must emphatically act according to culture rather than according to nature.
claims.source_passage: To go 'from A to Z' you must emphatically do what comes culturally.

claims.text: Society should return to the metaphor of the 'blank slate' for schooling because it aligns with the scientific truth of how cultural lore and literacy are acquired.
claims.source_passage: Once we acknowledge that scientific truth, perhaps we will be sensible enough to go back to his metaphor of the blank slate for most of our schooling.

evidence.description: Historical trend of declining verbal SAT scores since the 1940s.
Shift in physical classroom layout from desks in rows facing the teacher to tables and moveable chairs where children face each other.
M. H. Abrams' historical analysis of the shift from the 'Mirror' to the 'Lamp' in literary theory.
evidence.source_reference: Abrams, M. H. (1963). The Mirror and the Lamp.

evidence.description: Historical high reading scores when the 'mirror' model (teacher-centered, rows of desks) was prevalent.
The popular song 'Doin' What Comes Naturally' (1940s) as evidence of the cultural shift toward Romantic naturalism in the US.
The historical existence of diverse, non-natural writing systems (Roman, Greek, Hebrew, Persian, Chinese) as evidence that literacy is cultural, not innate.
T.E. Hulme's definition of Romanticism as 'spilt religion' used to characterize the fervor of educational romantics.
William Wordsworth's poetry used to illustrate the Romantic hostility toward intellectual dissection and formal science.

concepts.author_definition: A metaphor for education as an induction into external cultural reality, language, and the mores of national citizenship.
concepts.common_alternative: Accurately reflecting the world as it is.
concepts.source_passage: Our Enlightenment educational tradition had offered the child a direct, explicit induction into the language and the mores of national citizenship—a mirror.

concepts.author_definition: A metaphor for education as the illumination of a child's innate, individual genius and God-given nature.
concepts.common_alternative: A light that reveals or expresses internal thoughts.
concepts.source_passage: It described the literary shift from accurately mirroring the world as it is to illuminating the world with the lamp of one’s God-given individual genius.

concepts.author_definition: A botanical metaphor for the child's mind as an entity that grows and unfolds naturally from within, according to its innate nature.
A Lockean metaphor for the child's mind at birth, lacking innate ideas and ready to receive impressions of national language and culture.
A characterization of Romanticism as a secularized religious impulse that explains the ideological intensity of progressive educators.
The view that education is the unfolding of an innate, biological plan within the child, requiring only a supportive environment rather than external direction.
A specific philosophical characterization of John Dewey, blending Hegelian idealism with Darwinian evolutionary thought to justify child-centered education.
A standardized, common language system that requires explicit, whole-class instruction and shared background knowledge to master.
A term coined by Wordsworth to describe the analytical, scientific intervention that Romantics believe interferes with a child's natural growth.
The specific cultural knowledge and linguistic particularities that are not innate and must be inscribed upon the 'blank slate' of the child's mind.

cases.name: United States 1940s Transition
cases.description: The decade where the US moved from teacher-centered Enlightenment schooling to child-centered Romanticism, fueled by wartime confidence and the success of becoming the 'arsenal of democracy.'
key_passages: In that decade our move from teacher-centered to child-centered education started off in a big way.
It was then that American schooling departed from its intellectual roots in John Locke and the Enlightenment to adopt the Romantic approach to education.

cases.name: The US Educational Shift of the 1940s
cases.description: The period when the United States transitioned from an Enlightenment-based view of schooling (modeled on the blank slate) to a Romantic view (modeled on natural growth). This shift was reflected both in pedagogical theory and popular culture (e.g., hit songs).
key_passages: After the 1940s in the United States, Romanticism increasingly replaced our earlier Enlightenment ideas about schooling.
In the 1940s we took the plunge. Our American version of romanticism wasn’t as earnest as Wordsworth’s, but millions of Americans in the 1940s sang a song whose refrain invoked nature.

thinkers.name: John Locke
thinkers.key_idea: The human mind at birth is a blank slate (tabula rasa) without innate ideas, receptive to cultural and linguistic instruction.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch aligns the founding of the US with Lockean Enlightenment principles, which he argues are superior to the Romantic alternative.
key_passages: Initially, we adopted the teaching of John Locke, who held that children are born not with a lamp but with a mirror—in his metaphor it was a “blank slate” ready to have inscribed the national language and culture.

thinkers.name: M. H. Abrams
thinkers.key_idea: The intellectual transition from the Enlightenment to Romanticism can be understood through the metaphors of the Mirror (reflection) and the Lamp (expression).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch applies Abrams' literary-historical analysis of metaphors to the history of educational philosophy.
key_passages: One fine historian of the general subject, M. H. Abrams, published a famous book in 1963 entitled The Mirror and the Lamp. It described the literary shift from accurately mirroring the world as it is to illuminating the world with the lamp of one’s God-given individual genius.

thinkers.name: John Dewey
thinkers.key_idea: A 'Hegel-Darwin Romantic' who resuscitated the idea of innate ideas and natural growth in modern school theory.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch identifies Dewey as the primary modern theorist responsible for the shift toward Romantic, botanical metaphors in schooling.
key_passages: But innate ideas were resuscitated by the romantics—including by John Dewey, our most influential modern school theorist, who was a Hegel-Darwin Romantic

thinkers.name: William Wordsworth
thinkers.key_idea: The soul enters life with innate divinity and natural potential ('trailing clouds of glory').
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Wordsworth to illustrate the Romantic religious and philosophical roots of the 'seedling' and 'lamp' metaphors.
key_passages: “Fair seedtime had my soul” said the archetypal romantic, Wordsworth.

thinkers.name: T. E. Hulme
thinkers.engagement: Uses Hulme's definition to explain the dogmatic and fervent nature of child-centered educators.
key_passages: T. E. Hulme once gave a very succinct and profound definition of Romanticism as “spilt [spilled] religion.”

actors.name: John Locke
actors.context: Founder of the Enlightenment 'blank slate' tradition the author favors.

actors.name: John Dewey
actors.context: Influential theorist who shifted US schools toward Romanticism.

actors.name: M. H. Abrams
actors.context: Historian used to frame the shift in educational metaphors.

actors.name: Progressive Educators
actors.context: The group that applied botanical metaphors and child-centered ideas after the 1940s.

actors.name: Post-1940s Romantic Educators
actors.context: The group responsible for replacing instruction-based models with natural-growth models in American schools.

actors.name: Ethel Merman
actors.context: One of several popular singers cited to demonstrate the cultural dominance of the 'naturalist' metaphor in the 1940s.

objections_raised.objection: Shared knowledge instruction and desks in rows (the Mirror) might suppress individual growth or creativity.
objections_raised.response: Early mastery of shared language paradoxically enables more effective individuality and higher success later on.

objections_raised.objection: Education should be a natural growth from within, following a child's innate impulses, rather than 'artificial' external instruction.
objections_raised.response: Literacy and culture are not natural or innate; they are cultural constructs that cannot be acquired through 'natural growth.' Science supports the 'blank slate' model for these domains.
